summary: I was born in Durant, Oklahoma, right at the beginning of the Great Depression. We lived in a little stucco house on East 13th Street, near the railroad tracks at the edge of town. My father worked at a bank there before moving into insurance. I left Durant when I was five years old, so I don't remember much about it, but it was my first home.

Reba McEntire is one of the most famous people from Durant, Oklahoma, where I was born. She's a well-known actress and singer, and her fame stands out in that small town. I remember mentioning her when talking about notable people from Durant, alongside Dennis Rodman.

Dennis Rodman, the colorful NBA basketball player, also hails from Durant, Oklahoma, my birthplace. It's interesting to think about such a vibrant personality coming from the same small town as me. We often mentioned him when discussing famous Durant natives.

I was born right at the start of the Great Depression and lived through most of its nine years. I remember people coming around asking for food, and my mother would feed them even though we didn't have extra money ourselves. We were never hungry, but money was always tight during those hard times.
cues: born at start of Great Depression

summary: My mother was born near Idabel, Oklahoma, on a subsistence farm where they grew and made almost everything they used. My father was also born in Idabel, and they actually met in high school there. It was a small town with a farming background that shaped my parents' early lives.

My father's family originally came from Arkansas, specifically from a place called Delight. They moved to Oklahoma, where my parents eventually met and settled. Arkansas was part of my family's roots before they established themselves in Oklahoma.

When I was five, my family moved to Oklahoma City because my father took a new job as a general agent for the Equitable Life of Iowa. I started first grade there at Eugene Field Elementary School and continued my education through junior high and high school in Oklahoma City. It was where I grew up and spent my formative years.

entity: Equitable Life Assurance Society of the United States
summary: My father worked for the Equitable Life Assurance Society of the United States, specifically as the general agent for the state of Oklahoma after leaving banking. This job prompted our move to Oklahoma City. It was a significant part of his career and our family's stability during those years.

I started my formal education at Eugene Field Elementary School in Oklahoma City, beginning with first grade. It was my first school after moving from Durant. This school marked the start of my academic journey.

After moving to a new house further out in Oklahoma City, I attended Hawthorne Elementary School. Our family name, Brock, was known there for many years. It was part of my early schooling before junior high.
I went to Taft Junior High School in Oklahoma City after elementary school. It was the next step in my education before high school. I continued to grow academically and socially during those years.

I attended Classen High School, which was known for its exceptional courses and rigorous academics. I graduated from there in the spring of 1948. It was a great school that offered many opportunities and helped shape my interests, especially in history and geology.

I vividly remember December 7, 1941, the day of the attack on Pearl Harbor. We were living on West 13th Street in Oklahoma City, and the streets were unpaved then. I recall hearing the newsboys and cars shouting about the attack, which was the only 'Extra' newspaper I ever witnessed. It had a big impact on my family and community, with some relatives and friends joining the army soon after.

I attended the University of Oklahoma starting in the fall of 1948, mainly because it was affordable and where everyone else went. I joined the Delta Tau Delta fraternity and majored in business initially before switching to geological engineering. It was a fine school where I had a good experience and graduated in January 1953.

While at the University of Oklahoma, I joined the Delta Tau Delta fraternity. It was part of my college social life and connected me with many friends. Some fraternity brothers later served in the military, and sadly, a few died during the Cold War.

Bud Wilkinson became the football coach at the University of Oklahoma when I was a sophomore. I was lucky to attend during his era, and I never saw him lose a home game in five seasons. He was a man everyone admired and respected, and his legacy extended beyond coaching into broadcasting with ABC.

Bud Wilkinson, the famed University of Oklahoma football coach, later became a commentator on ABC. It was interesting to see someone from my college days transition into national broadcasting. ABC was part of that next chapter in his career.

After graduating in January 1953, I started working for Shell Oil Company. This was just before I was called up for military service in the Korean War. Shell was my first employer in the business world, aligning with my engineering degree.

I earned a commission through the Army Reserve Officers' Training Corps while at the University of Oklahoma. This prepared me for military service, and within a month of graduating, I was called up to serve in Korea. ROTC was an important part of my transition from college to the army.

I was called up near the end of the Korean War and arrived in Korea in late 1954, after the armistice. Although I didn't see combat, I served in the 13th Field Artillery Battalion, 24th Division, stationed north of the 38th parallel on Heartbreak Ridge. It was a tense time, but I was fortunate not to be involved in fighting.
cues: called up near end of war

summary: During my service in Korea, we were stationed just north of the 38th parallel, which was the dividing line between North and South Korea. Our position was on Heartbreak Ridge, within the demilitarized zone established after the war. North Korea was the adversary across the valley from us.

Our unit was positioned just north of the 38th parallel, facing South Korea across the valley. The war had ended, but the division between North and South Korea was very clear. We were part of the forces maintaining the ceasefire and peace in the area.

The 38th parallel north was the well-known dividing line between North and South Korea during my military service. We were stationed just north of this line on Heartbreak Ridge, which was part of the demilitarized zone. Everyone in Korea was very aware of this boundary.

I was stationed on Heartbreak Ridge during my time in Korea, which was a very mountainous area with a valley separating us from the enemy. It was a famous battle site, and our sector included Heartbreak Ridge on the left, Sandbag Castle in the center, and The Punch Bowl on the right. Although the fighting had ended by the time I arrived, it was a significant place in the war's history.

I served in the 13th Field Artillery Battalion, part of the 24th Infantry Division, during my time in Korea. I held several roles including company executive officer, battalion reconnaissance survey officer, and fire direction officer. They recognized my leadership abilities and gave me important responsibilities.

My unit was part of the 24th Infantry Division during the Korean War. We were stationed on Heartbreak Ridge, north of the 38th parallel. The division played a key role in maintaining the ceasefire and holding the line in that sector.

Sandbag Castle was the central part of our sector in Korea, situated between Heartbreak Ridge on the left and The Punch Bowl on the right. It was one of the key defensive positions in the mountainous terrain where we were stationed. I often mention it when describing my service area.
cues: between Heartbreak Ridge and The Punch Bowl

summary: The Punch Bowl was the rightmost part of our sector in Korea during my service. Along with Heartbreak Ridge and Sandbag Castle, it formed the front line positions in a very mountainous area. It was a well-known location in the Korean War landscape.
cues: alongside Heartbreak Ridge and Sandbag Castle
